title: Understanding Proximal Policy Optimization
description: A deep dive into the PPO algorithm — from policy gradients to clipped surrogate objectives and practical implementation details.
content:
## Understanding Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)

PPO is one of the most widely used reinforcement learning algorithms. This note breaks down the key ideas.

### Background: Policy Gradient Methods

Policy gradient methods optimize a parameterized policy $\pi_\theta(a|s)$ by estimating the gradient of the expected return:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a|s) \cdot A(s, a)]$$

where $A(s, a)$ is the advantage function.

### The Problem with Vanilla Policy Gradients

- **Large updates** can destroy a good policy
- **Sample efficiency** is poor
- No guarantee of monotonic improvement

### TRPO: The Precursor

TRPO constrains the KL divergence between old and new policies. Effective but computationally expensive due to conjugate gradient optimization.

### PPO: Clipped Surrogate Objective

PPO simplifies TRPO with a clipped objective:

```
L_CLIP(θ) = E[min(r_t(θ) · A_t, clip(r_t(θ), 1-ε, 1+ε) · A_t)]
```

where `r_t(θ) = π_θ(a|s) / π_θ_old(a|s)` is the probability ratio.

### Key Hyperparameters

| Parameter | Typical Value | Effect |
|-----------|--------------|--------|
| ε (clip range) | 0.1 - 0.2 | Controls update size |
| Learning rate | 3e-4 | Step size |
| GAE λ | 0.95 | Bias-variance tradeoff |
| Epochs per update | 3 - 10 | Reuse of collected data |

### Implementation Tips

1. **Normalize advantages** within each minibatch
2. **Use GAE** (Generalized Advantage Estimation) for lower variance
3. **Entropy bonus** prevents premature convergence
4. **Value function clipping** stabilizes training

### References

- Schulman et al., "Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms" (2017)
- Stable-Baselines3 implementation

title: YOLO Object Detection — Architecture Notes
description: Notes on the YOLO family of object detectors, from YOLOv1 through YOLOv8, covering architectural evolution and deployment strategies.
content:
## YOLO: You Only Look Once

Notes on the evolution of the YOLO family of real-time object detectors.

### YOLOv1 (2016)

- Single-shot detection: divides image into S×S grid
- Each cell predicts B bounding boxes + confidence + C class probabilities
- Revolutionary speed but limited accuracy

### YOLOv3 (2018)

- Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) for multi-scale detection
- Darknet-53 backbone
- Binary cross-entropy for multi-label classification

### YOLOv5 (2020)

- PyTorch-native implementation (Ultralytics)
- Automatic anchor computation
- Mosaic data augmentation
- Much easier to train and deploy

### YOLOv8 (2023)

- Anchor-free detection head
- Decoupled head (separate classification and regression)
- Task-specific heads: detection, segmentation, pose, classification
- State-of-the-art speed/accuracy tradeoff

### Deployment Considerations

- **TensorRT** for NVIDIA GPU inference
- **ONNX Runtime** for cross-platform deployment
- **TFLite** for mobile and edge devices
- **OpenVINO** for Intel hardware

### Key Takeaway

YOLO's evolution shows the trend from hand-crafted components (anchors, NMS) toward learned, end-to-end architectures.

title: Graph Neural Networks — A Primer
description: An introduction to graph neural networks covering message passing, common architectures (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE), and applications in spatial data.
content:
## Graph Neural Networks

A primer on GNNs and their application to non-Euclidean data.

### Why Graphs?

Many real-world systems are naturally represented as graphs:
- Social networks
- Molecular structures
- Transportation networks
- Terrain and geological maps

CNNs operate on regular grids. GNNs generalize convolution to arbitrary graph structures.

### Message Passing Framework

Most GNNs follow the message passing paradigm:

1. **Aggregate**: Collect messages from neighbors
2. **Update**: Combine aggregated message with node's own features
3. **Repeat**: Stack multiple layers for multi-hop reasoning

### Common Architectures

**GCN (Graph Convolutional Network)**
- Spectral approach simplified to spatial
- Simple mean aggregation with normalization

**GAT (Graph Attention Network)**
- Learned attention weights over neighbors
- Different neighbors contribute differently

**GraphSAGE**
- Sampling-based approach for large graphs
- Inductive: can generalize to unseen nodes

### Applications in My Research

- **Landslide susceptibility**: Terrain units as nodes, geological adjacency as edges
- **Environmental monitoring**: Sensor networks as spatial graphs

### Tools

- PyTorch Geometric
- DGL (Deep Graph Library)
- NetworkX (graph construction and analysis)

title: NeurIPS 2024 Reading Notes
description: Selected reading notes from NeurIPS 2024 papers on reinforcement learning, graph learning, and foundation models.
content:
## NeurIPS 2024 — Selected Reading Notes

Key papers and takeaways from NeurIPS 2024.

### Theme 1: Foundation Models Meet RL

Several papers explored using LLM/VLM representations for RL:
- Pre-trained vision encoders as state representations
- Language-conditioned reward shaping
- Foundation models as world models

### Theme 2: Graph Learning at Scale

- New sampling strategies for billion-node graphs
- Graph transformers competing with message-passing GNNs
- Temporal graph networks for dynamic systems

### Theme 3: AI for Science

- Growing track on ML for earth sciences
- Climate modeling with neural operators
- Molecular property prediction improvements

### Papers to Read in Detail

- [ ] "Scaling Graph Neural Networks to Billion Nodes"
- [ ] "Language-Guided Reinforcement Learning"
- [ ] "Neural Operators for Climate Downscaling"
- [ ] "Efficient Fine-tuning of Foundation Models for Robotics"

### Personal Takeaways

1. The gap between foundation models and RL is closing rapidly
2. Graph learning is moving toward transformer-like architectures
3. AI for science is becoming a first-class research area

title: Meta Learning — Learning to Learn
description: Notes on meta-learning approaches including MAML, Prototypical Networks, and their applications in few-shot learning scenarios.
content:
## Meta Learning: Learning to Learn

Meta-learning algorithms learn how to learn efficiently from limited data.

### Core Idea

Instead of learning a single task, learn a learning algorithm that can quickly adapt to new tasks from few examples.

### Key Approaches

**MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning)**
- Learns an initialization that can be fine-tuned in few gradient steps
- Task-agnostic: works with any differentiable model
- Inner loop: task-specific adaptation
- Outer loop: meta-parameter optimization

**Prototypical Networks**
- Learn an embedding space where classification is nearest-neighbor
- Class prototype = mean of support set embeddings
- Simple and effective for few-shot classification

**Matching Networks**
- Attention-based approach over support set
- Contextual embeddings using bi-LSTM

### Applications

- Few-shot image classification
- Quick robot adaptation
- Drug discovery with limited data
- Low-resource NLP

### Challenges

- Task distribution design
- Computational cost of bi-level optimization
- Generalization to out-of-distribution tasks

### Connection to My Research

Meta-learning could enable RL agents to quickly adapt to new environments — relevant to sim-to-real transfer in my navigation work.
